other_observers: Jackson Trappett (link to his photos on FLICKR is provided below), Art Dahl, Vic Zerbi, Lee Stigen, Ron Lambeth, Mr. and Mrs. Bob Wilson, Bob Bradley, Cecilia Barr, Dave and Dianne Trappett;
specific_location: east end of the Redlands, at 214 Country Club Park Road, locked onto a row of 15 pines along street near the mailbox labeled as 214
description: NOTES TAKEN 06 FEB - Referring to pg 311 of Big Sibley, note the juvie plumages shown for RNSA (through October) vs. YB (through March). Our bird was overall brownish with a small bit of red on the crown/forehead, striped facial pattern wasn’t strong like an adult RN/YB, was more like the juvies, except for a fairly strong black border above the throat (which was not completely white but smudgy-buffy), no red visible on the throat, and a few flecks of black were visible below the light throat area where adult RN/YB show strongly black...  NOTES TAKEN 08 FEB – we got longer, closer looks today in the same row of pines. Overall brownish-grayish bird with weak facial stripe pattern and the throat has darkish margins (not as black as I previously described). The red patch on the fore-crown seems more pronounced compared to the views we had two days ago, and is about the only adult-appearing bit of plumage. Whitish coverts apparent, and the back was overall splotchy/barred.Behaviors: this bird is often sitting still, locked in place on the trunk of a single pine tree, but at times actively forages and moves vertically on a given tree, sometimes between trees, seldom departs this row of pines. Prior encounters with YBSA in eastern US (and in TX in winter) surprised me how accommodating YBSA is at close range (like this bird is) vs. the shyness of RNSA. No vocals.Call: none, heard foraging on occasion, but not drummingPlumage: mostly juvenile, transitioning into adul
photographer: Photographer: Jackson Trappett, Jackson Trappett, Jackson Trappett, Jackson Trappett, Jackson Trappett
